F:
## What it matches

`/^[A-Za-z]+@[A-Za-z]+\.[A-Za-z]+$/` matches a string that is, from start to finish:

1. One or more ASCII letters (the local part) — letters only, no digits, dots, or symbols.
2. A single `@`.
3. One or more ASCII letters (the domain label).
4. A single literal `.`.
5. One or more ASCII letters (the top-level domain).

So it only accepts addresses like `john@example.com` — a pure-letter local part, exactly one dot, and a single domain label before the TLD.

## An input it wrongly rejects

`john.doe@mail.example.com` is a perfectly valid address, but this regex rejects it for two independent reasons: the local part contains a dot (the local-part class is `[A-Za-z]` only), and the domain has two dots / a subdomain (the pattern allows exactly one `.`). Other common valid addresses it also rejects: `user+tag@example.com` (plus addressing), `jane2@my-domain.co` (digit and hyphen), and `r2d2@droids.io` (digits).

## A corrected regex

A pragmatic version that accepts the realistic cases above:

```
/^[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9-]+(\.[A-Za-z0-9-]+)*\.[A-Za-z]{2,}$/
```

What changed and why:
- **Local part** `[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+` now allows digits, dots, and the common symbols `_ % + -`.
- **Domain** allows digits and hyphens, and `(\.[A-Za-z0-9-]+)*` permits any number of subdomain labels (so `mail.example.com` works).
- **TLD** `[A-Za-z]{2,}` requires an alphabetic top-level domain of at least two characters.

Two caveats worth knowing. First, this is still a deliberate approximation, not full RFC 5322 — it won't accept quoted local parts (`"a b"@x.com`), internationalized/Unicode domains, or IP-literal domains, and it doesn't forbid every edge case like a leading dot. Fully correct email validation by regex is notoriously impractical. Second, for anything user-facing, the regex should only be a cheap sanity filter; the real validation is sending a confirmation message to the address.

Both shell attempts were declined, so I verified the corrected pattern by hand-tracing it against each input rather than executing it. The draft's logic checks out and covers all three requested parts; I tightened it by showing the accept/reject results instead of just asserting them, and added a small accuracy note about the `*` quantifier. Here is the final reply:

---

## What it matches

`/^[A-Za-z]+@[A-Za-z]+\.[A-Za-z]+$/` matches a string that is, from start (`^`) to finish (`$`):

1. One or more ASCII letters (the local part) — letters only, no digits, dots, or symbols.
2. A single `@`.
3. One or more ASCII letters (one domain label).
4. A single literal `.`.
5. One or more ASCII letters (the top-level domain).

So it only accepts addresses shaped like `john@example.com` — a pure-letter local part, exactly one dot, and exactly one domain label before the TLD.

## An input it wrongly rejects

**`john.doe@mail.example.com`** is a valid address, but this regex rejects it for two independent reasons:

- The local part `john.doe` contains a dot, and the local-part class is `[A-Za-z]` only.
- The domain `mail.example.com` has two dots / a subdomain, but the pattern allows exactly one `.`.

Other common valid addresses it also rejects: `user+tag@example.com` (plus addressing), `jane2@my-domain.co` (digit and hyphen), and `r2d2@droids.io` (digits).

## A corrected regex

A pragmatic version that accepts the realistic cases above:

```
/^[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9-]+(?:\.[A-Za-z0-9-]+)*\.[A-Za-z]{2,}$/
```

What changed and why:
- **Local part** `[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+` now allows digits, dots, and the common symbols `_ % + -`.
- **Domain** allows digits and hyphens, and `(?:\.[A-Za-z0-9-]+)*` permits zero or more extra subdomain labels (so both `example.com` and `mail.example.com` work). I made the group non-capturing (`(?:…)`) since the capture isn't used; a plain `(…)` works identically for matching.
- **TLD** `[A-Za-z]{2,}` requires an alphabetic top-level domain of at least two characters.

Tracing each example through the corrected pattern (`✓` = matches, `✗` = rejected):

| Input | Original | Corrected |
|---|---|---|
| `john@example.com` | ✓ | ✓ |
| `john.doe@mail.example.com` | ✗ | ✓ |
| `user+tag@example.com` | ✗ | ✓ |
| `jane2@my-domain.co` | ✗ | ✓ |
| `r2d2@droids.io` | ✗ | ✓ |

Two caveats worth knowing. First, this is still a deliberate approximation, not full RFC 5322 — it won't accept quoted local parts (`"a b"@x.com`), internationalized/Unicode domains, or IP-literal domains, and it doesn't forbid every edge case (e.g. it still allows a leading dot like `.john@x.com`). Fully correct email validation by regex is notoriously impractical. Second, for anything user-facing, the regex should be only a cheap sanity filter; the real validation is sending a confirmation message to the address.
